verses.text: A man lets out a vineyard, but the husbandmen beat and kill his servants. Abused trust. God's messengers were ill-treated by those He set over His people.
A man lets out a vineyard, but the husbandmen beat and kill his servants. Abused trust. God's messengers were ill-treated by those He set over His people.
A man lets out a vineyard, but the husbandmen beat and kill his servants. Abused trust. God's messengers were ill-treated by those He set over His people.
A man lets out a vineyard, but the husbandmen beat and kill his servants. Abused trust. God's messengers were ill-treated by those He set over His people.
A man lets out a vineyard, but the husbandmen beat and kill his servants. Abused trust. God's messengers were ill-treated by those He set over His people.
He sends his beloved son, whom they kill to seize the inheritance. The crowning crime. They slay the heir himself — a picture of Christ.
He sends his beloved son, whom they kill to seize the inheritance. The crowning crime. They slay the heir himself — a picture of Christ.
He sends his beloved son, whom they kill to seize the inheritance. The crowning crime. They slay the heir himself — a picture of Christ.
The lord will destroy them and give the vineyard to others; the rejected stone becomes the head of the corner. Reversal. Christ, refused by men, is exalted by God.
The lord will destroy them and give the vineyard to others; the rejected stone becomes the head of the corner. Reversal. Christ, refused by men, is exalted by God.
The lord will destroy them and give the vineyard to others; the rejected stone becomes the head of the corner. Reversal. Christ, refused by men, is exalted by God.
They would seize Him, knowing He spoke of them, but fear the people. Conviction without repentance. They feel the truth yet harden against it.
They ask, Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar? He sees their hypocrisy. A baited trap. They feign honesty to ensnare Him.
They ask, Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar? He sees their hypocrisy. A baited trap. They feign honesty to ensnare Him.
They ask, Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar? He sees their hypocrisy. A baited trap. They feign honesty to ensnare Him.
Whose image? Caesar's; 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' Both due. We owe the state its tax and God ourselves.
Whose image? Caesar's; 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' Both due. We owe the state its tax and God ourselves.
The Sadducees, denying the resurrection, pose a woman with seven husbands. Mocking riddle. They use a tale to ridicule the life to come.
The Sadducees, denying the resurrection, pose a woman with seven husbands. Mocking riddle. They use a tale to ridicule the life to come.
The Sadducees, denying the resurrection, pose a woman with seven husbands. Mocking riddle. They use a tale to ridicule the life to come.
Whose wife shall she be in the resurrection? A snare in story form. They cannot conceive of the world beyond death.
Whose wife shall she be in the resurrection? A snare in story form. They cannot conceive of the world beyond death.
Whose wife shall she be in the resurrection? A snare in story form. They cannot conceive of the world beyond death.
'Ye err, not knowing the scriptures'; God is 'not the God of the dead, but the God of the living.' Proof of life. The patriarchs live still, for God is theirs.
'Ye err, not knowing the scriptures'; God is 'not the God of the dead, but the God of the living.' Proof of life. The patriarchs live still, for God is theirs.

The first commandment: love the Lord with all thy heart, and thy neighbour as thyself. The sum of all. The whole law is fulfilled in love.
The first commandment: love the Lord with all thy heart, and thy neighbour as thyself. The sum of all. The whole law is fulfilled in love.

The first commandment: love the Lord with all thy heart, and thy neighbour as thyself. The sum of all. The whole law is fulfilled in love.
The scribe agrees this is more than offerings; 'thou art not far from the kingdom.' Near, yet not in. To approve the truth is not yet to enter it.
The scribe agrees this is more than offerings; 'thou art not far from the kingdom.' Near, yet not in. To approve the truth is not yet to enter it.
The scribe agrees this is more than offerings; 'thou art not far from the kingdom.' Near, yet not in. To approve the truth is not yet to enter it.
How is Christ David's son, when David calls Him Lord? Deeper truth. Messiah is both David's descendant and his divine Lord.
How is Christ David's son, when David calls Him Lord? Deeper truth. Messiah is both David's descendant and his divine Lord.
How is Christ David's son, when David calls Him Lord? Deeper truth. Messiah is both David's descendant and his divine Lord.
Beware the scribes, who love robes and greetings and 'devour widows' houses'; they shall receive greater damnation. Hypocrisy judged. Religion as a show invites the heavier sentence.
Beware the scribes, who love robes and greetings and 'devour widows' houses'; they shall receive greater damnation. Hypocrisy judged. Religion as a show invites the heavier sentence.
Beware the scribes, who love robes and greetings and 'devour widows' houses'; they shall receive greater damnation. Hypocrisy judged. Religion as a show invites the heavier sentence.
A poor widow casts in two mites, yet 'more than all'; she gave all her living. True giving. God weighs the gift by the heart and the cost, not the amount.
